votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the prefix sums logic and checks if any of the balances are less than zero, exactly matching the Coq specification.
The Python code correctly implements the prefix sums logic and checks if any of the balances fall below zero, exactly matching the Coq specification.
The Python code correctly implements the prefix sums logic and checks if any balance is below zero, matching the Coq specification.

The Python prefix_sums computes the same list of running sums as the Coq Fixpoint. The Coq spec says result=true iff there exists a negative balance in the prefix sums. The Python code computes exists_negative as any(bal < 0 for bal in balances) and checks result == exists_negative. When result is True, this matches the Coq biconditional (True iff exists negative). When result is False, this also matches (False iff no negative exists). So for all concrete inputs (operations, result), the Python function returns the same boolean judgment as the Coq spec.

For concrete inputs, when result is a boolean, this returns True exactly when result matches the correct answer per the Coq spec (result=True iff some balance is negative, result=False iff no balance is negative), which agrees with the biconditional in the Coq Prop.
